coredata.dc:title: An immunosensor designed for polybrominated biphenyl detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between carbon dots and gold nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Bu, Dan
Zhuang, Huisheng
Yang, Guangxin
Ping, Xianxin
coredata.dc:description: A novel assembled immunosensor was prepared for determining 4,4′-dibrominated biphenyl (PBB15), a small-size persistent organic pollutant. The immunosensing approach is based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), which act as energy donor and acceptor, respectively. The immunosensor consists of a specific combination of CDs-conjugated PBB15 antigen and AuNPs-modified anti-PBB15 antibody, applied in a homogenous assay system. Results showed that in the absence of PBB15, FRET between CDs and the AuNPs resulted in fluorescence intensity quenching. By contrast, fluorescence intensity was recovered in the presence of PBB15 as CD-labeled antigen and PBB15 competitively combined with antibody on the surface of AuNPs. Fluorescence intensity was proportional to PBB15 concentration in the range of 0.05–4μg/mL, with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.039μg/mL. The advantages of homogenous over heterogeneous immunoassays include the absence of repetitive washing and incubation steps. In this proposed assay, the procedure is quite simple and could be accomplished within 1h. The immunosensor had excellent selectivity for PBB15 over other structural analogs. The proposed immunoassay was applied to determine PBB15 in biological samples, and the precision and recovery results were satisfactory. The high selectivity and short assay time of this designed immunosensor indicate its potential application in in situ and rapid detection. Furthermore, the strategy provided in this work could be conveniently followed to establish similar immunosensors for the rapid detection of small-sized organic pollutants with corresponding antigens and antibodies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Immunosensor
Polybrominated biphenyls (PBB)
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)
Carbon dots
In situ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon dots synthesized by microwave method
Preparation of PBB antigen-conjugated carbon dots (CDs-PBBAg)
Preparation of the anti-PBB antibody-conjugated AuNPs (AuNPs-antiPBBAb)
Sample preparation and detection by FRET-based immunosensor
Labeling rate and activity of prepared AuNPs-antiPBBAb
Binding studies of CDs-PBBAg and AuNPs-antiPBBAb
Optimization of detection conditions and method validation
Determination of PBB15 in sea-fish sample and recovery test

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of PBB15 hapten, immunogen and artificial antigen; (a) activated ester method (b) mixed anhydride method.
TEM images of (a) AuNPs, (b) AuNPs-antiPBBAb, and (c) their relative UV–vis spectra.
FT-IR spectroscopy of prepared carbon dots.
UV–vis spectrum of CDs and fluorescence spectrum of CDs, CDs-PBBAg.
Absorption spectrum of (a) AuNPs-antiPBBAb combined with (b) CD-PBBAg and dissociated by (c) PBB15.
Schematic illustration of the determination of PBB15 using FRET-based immunosensor for quenching experiment and competitive immunoassay.

Suitable operating conditions of FRET-based immunosensor and working curve; (a) the concentration of antigen and relative antibody; (b) incubation time; (c) different pH in buffer; (d) different of ion strength in buffer; (e) fluorescence spectra of FRET immunosensor in the presence of different concentrations of PBB15; (f) plot of relative fluorescence intensity (ΔF
=
F
−
F 0).

Specificity of FRET immunosensor; the concentration of each small size analyte (including PBB15) was 1μg/mL. In a typical experiment, the concentrations of AuNPs-antiPBBAb and CDs-PBBAg were 19μg/mL and 180μg/mL, respectively.
Immune performance comparison of anti-PBBAb with AuNPs-antiPBBAb.
The variability of intra-assay and inter-assay of the standard curve of BA-ELISA.
Recoveries of PBB15 from spiked samples.

head.ce:title: An immunosensor designed for polybrominated biphenyl detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between carbon dots and gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Monitoring and Evaluation, School of Environment Science and Technology, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, No.800, Dongchuan Road, Minghang district, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Monitoring and Evaluation, School of Environment Science and Technology, Shanghai Jiao Tong University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: East China Sea Fisheries Research Institute, No.300, Jungong Road, Yangpu district, Shanghai, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: East China Sea Fisheries Research Institute

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel assembled immunosensor was prepared for determining 4,4′-dibrominated biphenyl (PBB15), a small-size persistent organic pollutant. The immunosensing approach is based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), which act as energy donor and acceptor, respectively. The immunosensor consists of a specific combination of CDs-conjugated PBB15 antigen and AuNPs-modified anti-PBB15 antibody, applied in a homogenous assay system. Results showed that in the absence of PBB15, FRET between CDs and the AuNPs resulted in fluorescence intensity quenching. By contrast, fluorescence intensity was recovered in the presence of PBB15 as CD-labeled antigen and PBB15 competitively combined with antibody on the surface of AuNPs. Fluorescence intensity was proportional to PBB15 concentration in the range of 0.05–4μg/mL, with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.039μg/mL. The advantages of homogenous over heterogeneous immunoassays include the absence of repetitive washing and incubation steps. In this proposed assay, the procedure is quite simple and could be accomplished within 1h. The immunosensor had excellent selectivity for PBB15 over other structural analogs. The proposed immunoassay was applied to determine PBB15 in biological samples, and the precision and recovery results were satisfactory. The high selectivity and short assay time of this designed immunosensor indicate its potential application in in situ and rapid detection. Furthermore, the strategy provided in this work could be conveniently followed to establish similar immunosensors for the rapid detection of small-sized organic pollutants with corresponding antigens and antibodies.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a sensitive spectroscopic probe used to investigate changes in molecular structure; it involves a non-radiative transfer process of resonant fluorescence energy from an excited fluorophore serving as a donor to a ground-state acceptor via long-range dipole–dipole interactions [1]. An efficient FRET process is mainly influenced by two factors: (1) the degree of overlap between the emission spectrum of donor and the absorption spectrum of acceptor and (2) the distance between the donor and acceptor molecules [2]. Optimal distances for efficient FRET are commonly in the range of 2–9nm [3]. Thus, experimental designs must consider decreasing the distance between the donor and the acceptor particles as much as possible. One of the most commonly used techniques involves incorporation of fluorescent donors and acceptors with biomolecules (such as antigens and antibodies) to design artificial immunosensors. Under this superior strategy, selective detection due to the specificity of biomolecules and appropriate distances comparable with the optimal 2–9nm FRET range may be simultaneously achieved. In the current study, an immunosensor, combining a recognition module based on biosensitive material and signal transducers, is proposed. The interaction between a target analyte and the bioreceptor produces a physicochemical perturbation on the nanobiosensor, which converts the perturbation into a measurable effect, e.g., an optical or electrical signal [4].

Several FRET-based immunosensors [5,6] have recently been developed for investigating or detecting biomolecules in vivo or in vitro. In these sensors, organic dyes and fluorescent proteins [6,7] were used as donors or acceptors, respectively. However, several drawbacks [8], such as fixed absorption/emission spectra, low stability, short observation time, and photobleaching, inhibit the performance of these sensors. To overcome these problems, quantum dot nanoparticles may be used as excellent resonant energy donors because of their high quantum yields, excellent photostability, large Stokes shift, narrow emission, and broad excitation [9,10]. Although FRET-based immunosensors feature various designs, they employ a comparable pattern of homogeneous competitive assay and could direct readout in solution after only an incubation step. Unfortunately, short assay times are often attained by sacrificing sensitivity and dynamic ranges, considering that a fast readout is sometimes more important than a wide dynamic range and high sensitivity in several application fields, e.g., in environmental pollution incidents and clinical point-of-care applications.

To the best of our knowledge, homogeneous immunosensors are usually reported to detect an abundance of biomacromolecules, and only a few [11] are used for small-size organic molecule determination. Thus in this study, by taking advantage of the principles of FRET and homogeneous immunoassay, we present a novel immunosensor for the rapid and selective detection of 4,4′-dibrominated biphenyl (PBB15), a typical persistent organic pollutant with endocrine-disrupting effects in biology [12]. In this proposed immunosensor, carbon dots (CDs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are used as fluorescence donors and acceptors, respectively. Two portions of this immunosensor, namely, CD-labeled PBB15 antigen (CDs-PBBAg) and AuNP-functionalized anti-PBB15 antibody (AuNPs-antiPBBAb), are first synthesized. The immunosensor is obtained by the reaction of CDs-PBBAg with AuNPs-antiPBBAb, during which an obvious FRET phenomenon can be observed in the absence of targets. Upon addition of PBB, the CDs-PBBAg is released from the surface of the AuNPs because of competitive immunoreactions, which results in the recovery of fluorescence intensity. This restored fluorescence intensity is proportional to the concentration of PBB15. The whole assay process does not require multiple time-consuming incubation, separation, and washing steps and could be rapidly accomplished within a very short time. The proposed immunosensor is appropriate for in-situ or rapid detection. The results of this study provide a valid model for establishing other immunosensors for the detection of small-sized organic molecules with relevant antigens and antibodies.

All reagents were of analytical grade unless specified otherwise. Chloroauric acid, polyethylene glycol 20000 (PEG-20000) and polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG-200) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Sodium citrate, glucose, succinic anhydride, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), n-butyl amine, acid chloride butyl acetate, coomassie brilliant blue G250, Tween 20 were purchased from Sinopharm (China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), egg albumin (OVA), goat anti-rabbit IgG/HRP, and Freund's complete adjuvant were purchased from Sangon Biotech. 4,4′-dibrominated biphenyl (4,4-DiBB IUPAC number 15) and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC-HCl) were obtained from J&K-Chemical Co. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; 10mmol/L sodium phosphate and 137mmol/L NaCl, pH 7.4) was prepared in the laboratory.

Immunoassay was carried out in 96-well black fluorescent microplates and fluorescence intensity of each well was measured by a multifunctional microplate reader (Varioskan Flash, Thermo, USA). The morphology and size of as-prepared nano-particles were observed on an analytical transmission electron microscope (JEM-2010HT, JEOL, Japan), using an accelerating voltage of 200kV. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-7504PC, Shanghai Xinmao Instrument Co., Ltd, China). Infrared spectra of the carbon dots were obtained by a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR-2 Nicolet 6700, Thermo, USA). And the fluorescence spectra were measured on a fluorescence photometer (RF5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan). All used water was purified by an ultra-pure water purifier (Milli-Q plus 185, Millipore, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots synthesized by microwave method
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized using a domestic microwave oven following the procedure described in the literature with minor modifications [13]. Briefly, 2.5g of glucose and 10mL of PEG-200 were added to 3mL of distilled water. Then, the solution was heated in microwave oven (630W) for 10min until a dark yellow solution was obtained. Finally, the yellow solution was purified and diluted with water.

AuNPs were prepared by reduction of sodium citrate [14]. Both HAuCl4 solution (100mL, 2.7×10−4 mol/L) and sodium citrate solution (20mL, 3.4×10−3 mol/L) were maintained at 80°C in a water bath. Subsequently, the sodium citrate solution was added to the HAuCl4 solution. The mixture was kept for 15min and then cooled down to room temperature.

To synthesize the PBB15 antigen and antibody, the PBB15 molecule was modified to conjugate with the carrier proteins bovine serum albumin and ovalbumin; this procedure was conducted according to the process described in our previous studies [15]. A branched chain with a terminal carboxyl group was introduced to the PBB15 molecule. Briefly, 20mmol of PBB15 and 20mmol of succinic anhydride were mixed under ice-cold water bath conditions. AlCl3 (50mmol) was added dropwise into the solution, and the reaction was allowed to react for 12h. After reaction, the suspension was washed using ice-cold HCl solution and dichloromethane to yield the PBB15 hapten. The activated ester [16] and mixed anhydride [17,18] methods were used to couple hapten with different carrier proteins. The routes for synthesizing PBB15 hapten, the artificial antigen, and the immunogen are shown in Fig. 1 .

The anti-PBB15 polyclonal antibody was prepared by immunizing New Zealand rabbits. Immunization was carried out using immunogen emulsified with complete/incomplete Freund's adjuvant. Anti-PBB15 antibody was separated from rabbit blood after 4 months of immunization. The obtained polyclonal antibody was purified and stored at −20°C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PBB antigen-conjugated carbon dots (CDs-PBBAg)
ce:section.ce:para: First, PBB artificial antigen was diluted with PBS to 1mg/mL. Equivoluminal PBB antigen and CDs solution were mixed, followed by addition of 0.5μM of 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride and N-hydroxy succinimide. The mixture was stirred slowly at 37°C for 1h. Tris (100mg) was added to occupy the unblocking sites on the CDs, and the resultant mixture was allowed to react at 37°C for another 1h. Excess unconjugated CDs were separated by centrifugation and washed thrice with PBS. The final precipitate was redissolved in 1.0mL of PBS and stored at 4°C until use. The protein content of the prepared CDs-PBBAg was measured using the Coomassie Brilliant Blue method, and a result of 0.54mg/mL was obtained.

The anti-PBB antibody was dialyzed against 0.005M NaCl for 12h at 4°C to remove excess salt. After dialysis, the antibody was obtained by centrifugation, and the precipitate was discarded. The pH of the colloidal gold suspension was adjusted to 8.5– 9.0 with 0.1MK2CO3.

Pre-treated AuNP collosol (1mL) was added into tubes. The anti-PBB antibody (7.8mg/mL) was adjusted to different dilutions (50×, 100×, 200×, 400×, 800×, 1600×, and 3200×) and then mixed with AuNP collosol. After vortex-mixing, the mixture was placed for 2h at room temperature, after which a few drops of 10% NaCl solution were added into each tube. Color changes in the solution were noted. A red hue indicates protein content reaching and/or exceeding the minimal dose, whereas a violet coloration indicates otherwise. The optimal dilution of anti-PBB antibody was determined to be 400×.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the anti-PBB antibody-conjugated AuNPs (AuNPs-antiPBBAb)
ce:section.ce:para: Anti-PBB antibody solution (19.5μg/mL) was added into 1mL of AuNP collosol and the solution was mechanically stirred for 1h. PEG-20000 at a final concentration of 0.05% was added to this solution to block the active sites on the protein molecule. After resting overnight at 4°C, the AuNPs-antiPBBAb was separated by centrifugation and washed thrice with PBS containing 0.5% PEG-20000. The concentration of AuNPs-antiPBBAb was 0.038mg/mL.

The activities of anti-PBB antibody and the prepared AuNPs-antiPBBAb were evaluated and compared by indirect competitive ELISA (ic-ELISA) in white polystyrene 96-well plates. Briefly, two kinds of antibodies were diluted to certain multiples and added into the wells, which were treated by coating and blocking prepensely. Goat anti-rabbit IgG/HRP, which was used to recognize antigens and catalyze substrates, was added as the second antibody. The final absorbance values were measured by a Multiskan MK3 ELISA reader (Thermo, USA) in dual-wavelength mode (450/630nm).

The labeling rate of obtained AuNPs-antiPBBAb can be calculated using Eq. (1)
(1)
P ( % ) =
C
sample
×
V
sample
−
C
supernate
×
V
supernate
C
sample
×
V
sample
× 100 %
where C sample and C supernate are the concentrations of the antibody and supernate, respectively, used to label the AuNPs and V sample and V supernate are the volumes of the antibody and supernate, respectively.

Immunoreactions were performed as follows: for quenching experiments, a predetermined amount of CDs-PBBAg and a serial dilution of the AuNPs-antiPBBAb were mixed followed by addition of PBS to a final volume of 200μL. The solution was incubated at 37°C for 1h. The fluorescence intensity was measured using a multifunctional microplate reader (λ Ex =280nm, bandwidth=10nm; λ Em =506nm, bandwidth=10nm). Quenched values were calculated as the difference of the unquenched fluorescence intensity (no AuNPs-antiPBBAb) and the intensity of the actual quenched fluorescence. In the competitive immunoassay, 100μL of 19μg/mL AuNPs-antiPBBAb and 50μL of 180μg/mL CDs-PBBAg were mixed in a well. Approximately 50μL of PBB standard (0.01–8μg/mL) or sample solution was subsequently added to the wells. The fluorescence intensity was measured after incubation at 37°C for 1h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation and detection by FRET-based immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: PBB is a type of brominative additive used as flame retardants, which could be easily released into the environment. Meanwhile, because of the chemical durability, recalcitrance, and high lipophilicity, PBBs can cause bioaccumulation and amplification effect in food chain. In this study, the fish (Larimichthys polyactis and hairtail) samples (East China Sea Fisheries Research Institute, Shanghai) were obtained and analyzed.

For further treatment, 1g of homogeneous fish meat sample, without lipid and connective tissue, was ultrasonically extracted twice with 2g of anhydrous sodium sulfate and 15mL of ethanol/phosphoric acid (200:1, v/v), followed by centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 30min. Supernatants were pooled and gently dried under vacuum, and the residue was dissolved in 1mL of acetonitrile. About 3mL of n-hexane saturated with acetonitrile was added to this mixture. After vortex shocking for 5min, the supernatant was filtered through a Florisil solid phase extraction column. The column was washed with methanol (2mL/min) and dried by nitrogen. The residue was dissolved in 1mL of MeOH. The prepared fish sample was analyzed using HPLC to confirm the absence of PBB15 before proceeding with the standard addition recovery experiments.

The prepared AuNPs and AuNPs-antiPBBAb were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and UV–vis spectroscopy, the results of which are shown in Fig. 2 . The particle size of the AuNPs was estimated to be 15nm from the TEM images, and an extinction coefficient of 3.6×108 Lmol−1 cm−1 was obtained. According to Beer's law [19], the concentration of the AuNP solution was estimated to be 2.09×10−9 mol/L. The surface plasmon resonance absorbance peak of the AuNPs appeared at 521nm. After anti-PBB antibody molecules attached to the surface of the AuNPs, the absorption peak of AuNPs-antiPBBAb slightly red-shifted from 521nm to 528nm. Another absorption peak appeared at 270nm; this peak represents a typical absorption peak of proteins.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was used to study the surface functional groups present on the CDs, and the results shown in Fig. 3 . The peaks at 1126 and 1260cm−1 are assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COC, respectively [20]. Peaks associated with the stretching vibrations of carboxylic (COO) and hydroxyl (OH) groups can be found at 1633 and 3450cm−1 [21]; these peaks confirm the presence of carboxyl groups. These functional groups offer surface modification opportunities for coupling CDs with protein.

The optical property of as-prepared CDs was studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The absorption peak of CDs emerged at 250–300nm and the maximum fluorescence peak emerged at 515nm. A blue shift can be observed after CDs were labeled onto the surface PBB-Ag, with a fluorescence peak occurring at 506nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in the text, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Labeling rate and activity of prepared AuNPs-antiPBBAb
ce:section.ce:para: The labeling rate of AuNPs-antiPBBAb was calculated using Eq. (1), and the result was 72.4%. The activity results of anti-PBBAb and AuNPs-antiPBBAb were compared with the results of ic-ELISA; Table 1 lists these results.

The absorbance values of two antibodies decreased with increasing dilution. Maximum dilution multiples of anti-PBBAb and AuNPs-antiPBBAb were 1:6000 and 1:4000, respectively. Although the activity of the AuNPs-antiPBBAb declined, the modified immunoglobulin still retained sufficient activity for the immunoassay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding studies of CDs-PBBAg and AuNPs-antiPBBAb
ce:section.ce:para: To study the binding interaction of CDs-PBBAg and AuNPs-antiPBBAb, the optical absorption spectra of AuNPs-antiPBBAb associated with CDs-PBBAg and PBB15 was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , a surface plasmon resonance peak of AuNPs-antiPBBAb appeared at 528nm. After CD-PBBAg was introduced to the solution, a red-shift to 534nm was observed. This shift may be ascribed to the aggregation of AuNPs-PBBAb. After addition of PBB15, AuNPs-PBBAb was dissociated from the surface of CDs-PBBAg because of competitive reactions. This dissociation is reflected as a blue-shift to the original spectrum of AuNPs-antiPBBAb. These results indicate that the interaction between AuNPs-antiPBBAb and CDs-PBBAg by means of specific bimolecular recognition is reversible and that PBB15 could partly dissociate AuNPs-antiPBBAb/CDs-PBBAg complexes. Therefore, the binding interaction is the most important factor to consider in establishing a FRET-based immunosensor. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)

The efficiency of FRET mostly depends on two factors: (1) spectral overlap between the emission of the donor and the absorption of the acceptor and (2) the distance between the donor and the acceptor. Some experimental studies have demonstrated that CDs are newly emerging fluorescence carbon nanomaterials that are suitable for use as fluorescent labels. AuNPs are excellent fluorescence superquenchers with a long-range nanoscale energy transfer properties [22–24]. The spectral overlap of CD emission and AuNP absorption provides a basis for FRET. In this study, CDs were used as a donor to label CDs-PBBAg; AuNPs were selected as an acceptor to modify AuNPs-antiPBBAb. The distance between the donor and acceptor in an antibody-antigen complex is in the range of 5–10nm, which is comparable with the optimal distance for efficient FRET (2–9nm).

The principle of the proposed FRET-based immunosensor is illustrated in Fig. 6 . In the absence of PBB15, CDs-PBBAg is combined with the antigen-binding site of the AuNPs-conjugated antibody. Upon excitation, CDs transfer their fluorescence energy through FRET to the quencher molecules because of the very close distance of the immunocomplex. Upon addition of PBB15, CDs-PBBAg is partly displaced from AuNPs-antiPBBAb because of competitive immunoreaction, which causes the disruption of FRET and creation of an optical signal through the restoration of CD fluorescence. The restored fluorescence intensity is proportional to the concentration of PBB15. Hence, the concentration of PBB15 may be quantified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions and method validation
ce:section.ce:para: According to the theory of homogeneous FRET immunoassay, FRET occurs as antigen-antibody complexes are formed. Some parameters that affect the antigen-antibody immunoreaction can further disrupt the fluorescence transfer efficiency. Thus, factors possible affecting FRET, such as concentration of the antigen and antibody, immunoreaction time, pH variation, and ion strength of buffers, were studied; the results are shown in Fig. 7 .

The fluorescence intensity of CDs-PBBAg decreased gradually with increasing AuNPs-antiPBBAb concentration. When the concentration of AuNPs-antiPBBAb and CDs-PBBAg were 19μg/mL and 180μg/mL, respectively, minimum fluorescence intensity was observed.

Assay time, one of the most important factors of a homogeneous assay, is discussed in this study. Fluorescence intensity measurement was initiated by mixing the donor and the acceptor solution. Fluorescence intensity decreased and remained at low stable levels after 1h, which indicates a tendency to complete immunoreactions on the AuNPs surface. The control experiment was conducted without addition of the acceptor. Results showed that fluorescence intensity was not altered. Therefore, the entire immunoassay could be accomplished within 1h.

The antigen-antibody immunoreaction, as well as fluorescence transfer efficiency, is easily affected by buffer pH. The fluorescence intensities of both CDs-PBBAg/antiPBBAb-AuNPs and CDs-PBBAg were measured under varying pH (2–12), and the results are shown in Fig. 7c. Buffer pH affected not only the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-PBBAg, but also the fluorescence quenching degree of the immunosensor. Maximal fluorescence quenching was achieved at a pH range of 6–8.

The immunoassay performance of the FRET-based immunosensor was determined under different ionic strengths; here, NaCl concentrations were varied from 0.05mol/L to 0.5mol/L. As shown in Fig. 7d, FRET quenching efficiency increased with increasing NaCl concentration. As the concentration of NaCl reached 0.2mol/L, maximal maximum quenching efficiency was obtained. The observed FRET efficiency increased to 58.8%, higher than the typical quenching efficiencies of homogeneous FRET assays, which normally range from 30% to 50%. Therefore, the quenching efficiency changed slightly with increasing ionic strength. Hence, the optimal ionic strength was selected 0.2mol/L.

Under optimum conditions, a series of PBB15 standard solutions (0.01–8μg/mL) was measured to determine the linearity between relative fluorescence intensity, ΔF (Δ F = F − F 0, where F and F 0 represent the fluorescent intensity of homogeneous FRET-based immunoassay in the presence and absence of PBB15) and PBB15 concentration. Results were shown in Fig. 7e and f. Fluorescent intensity gradually increased with increasing concentration of PBB15. The analysis for each point was performed thrice, and the mean was used to plot the calibration curve. A good linear relationship was found between ΔF and PBB15 concentration in the range of 0.05–4μg/mL. Linear regression analysis of the results yielded the following equations: ΔF =149C +37.24(R 2 =0.991). The detection limit (LOD) was 0.039μg/mL (3.3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of blank sample, and S is the slope of the calibration curve).

Intra-assay reproducibility was calculated from the results of six independent replicates in parallel, and inter-assay reproducibility was determined from separate assays over 5 days. Results of the intra-assay and inter-assay variations, which were below 12% and 17%, are shown in Table 2 . Although assay variation is higher than that in other heterogeneous immunoassays, this variation is expected for homogeneous immunoassays [25]. The real advantage of homogeneous immunoassay over heterogeneous one is the short assay time of the former; homogenous immunoassays usually take only 10–60min to complete [26–29]. Hence, the precision obtained in this work allows the semi-quantitative detection of PBB15.

To reveal the specificity of the proposed immunoassay, different structural analog, such as 3,4-dichlorobiphenyl, 3,4,4′-trichlorobiphenyl, tetrabromobisphenol, fluoranthene, biphenyl, and Tris(2,3-dibromopropyl) isocyanuratate, were added during optimal detection steps. In a typical experiment, the FRET immunosensor was incubated with 1μg/mL of each analog and the fluorescent intensities at 506nm were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 8 , only PBB15 led to enhanced fluorescence intensity; other small-sized analogs with concentrations similar to PBB15 failed to recover fluorescence signals. These results indicate that the FRET immunosensor possesses high selectivity for detecting PBB15.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PBB15 in sea-fish sample and recovery test
ce:section.ce:para: The recovery of samples spiked with the target analyte was calculated to assess the analytical performance of the designed immunosensor. Blank samples (determined by HPLC) were spiked with different PBB15 concentrations, and the recoveries were measured. Samples of 1g PBB15-free fish meat homogenate were spiked with 0.5, 1, or 2μg PBB15/g meat and extracted with the above treatment procedures. The theoretical PBB15 concentrations of the extracts were 0.5, 1, and 2μg/mL. Each sample was tested thrice to verify the analytical reproducibility. Results were shown in Table 3 . The average recoveries from two types of fish were in the range of 83.6–104.5% for Larimichthys polyactis and 86.5–98.2% for hairtail. The intra-assay coefficient of variations ranged from 6.7% to 11.2% and 7.3% to 12.5%, respectively.

A novel assembled FRET-based immunosensor CDs-PBBAg/antiPBBAb-AuNPs was designed to detect 4, 4′-bibronine diphenyl. In this immunosensor, CDs were used as a donor to label PBB15 antigen. AuNPs were selected as an acceptor to modify anti-PBB15 antibody. Excellent quenching efficiency between AuNPs and CDs, and the subsequent fluorescence recovery induced by competitive immunoreactions achieved high sensitivity and selectivity detection of the analyte. At optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the assembled immunosensor was proportional to the concentration of PBB15 in the range of 0.05–4μg/mL, with an LOD of 0.039μg/mL. Compared with conventional immunoassay, the proposed assay does not involve separation of reactants and repeated washing steps. Therefore, this assay is simple and time-saving; it could be accomplished within 1h. We believe that this novel FRET-based immunosensor provides a useful model for the in situ or rapid detection of other small-molecule organic analyte using corresponding antigens and relative antibodies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. 21177082), the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality in China (Key Project for Fundamental Research No. 09JC1407600), and the science and technology innovation special fund development projects of Shanghai Jiaotong University.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Materials for fluorescence resonance energy transfer analysis: beyond traditional donor-acceptor combinations
Luminescence resonance energy transfer sensors based on the assemblies of oppositely charged lanthanide/gold nanoparticles in aqueous solution
Nanoprobes and nanobiosensors for monitoring and imaging individual living cells
A homogeneous fluorescent sensor for human serum albumin
Green fluorescent protein (GFP): applications, structure, and related photophysical behavior
Fluorescein provides a resonance gate for FRET from conjugated polymers to DNA intercalated dyes
Luminescent rare earth nanomaterials for bioprobe applications
Förster resonance energy transfer investigations using quantum-dot fluorophores
Bi-enzyme sensor for phenolic compounds with fluorescent read-out
Determination of brominated flame retardants in environmental samples
Microwave synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with electrochemiluminescence properties
Controlled nucleation for the regulation of the particle size in monodisperse gold suspensions
Development of an analytical protocol for the determination of polybrominated biphenyls in water by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay
Hapten synthesis for enzyme linked immunoassay of the insecticide triazophos
Steroid protein conjugates I: preparation and characterization of bovine serum albumin with testosterone and with cortisone
Hapten concentrations in the circulation of animals actively immunized with hapten-protein conjugates
Colorimetric sensing of silver(I) and mercury(II) ions based on an assembly of Tween 20-stabilized gold nanoparticles
Synthesis of high-quality carbon nanodots from hydrophilic compounds: role of functional groups
Nanosized carbon particles from natural gas soot
Beyond superquenching hyper-efficient energy transfer from conjugated polymers to gold nanoparticles
Gold nanoparticles quench fluorescence by phase induced radiative rate suppression
Fluorescent lifetime quenching near d =1.5nm gold nanoparticles: probing NSET validity
Homogeneous fluorescence-based immunoassay detects antigens within 90 seconds
Time-resolved fluorescence resonance energy transfer assay for point-of-care testing of urinary albumin
Rapid and simple quantitation of methamphetamine by using a homogeneous time-resolved fluoroimmunoassay based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer from europium to Cy5
Homogeneous indirect fluorescence quenching immunoassay for the determination of low molecular weight substances
Homogeneous time-resolved fluoroimmunoassay of 3,5,3′-triiodo-l-thyronine in human serum by using europium fluorescence energy transfer

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dan Bu obtaining a masters degree at Donghua University, Shanghai in 2011; a PhD candidate at Shanghai Jiaotong University from 2011 to now. Research direction: the research of immunoassay methods for detecting persistent organic pollutants.

Huisheng Zhuang Prof. (Dr.) Zhuang graduated from chemistry department, Fuzhou University, and worked in Fuzhou University from 1988 to 2002; worked in Donghua University from 2002 to 2008; worked in Shanghai Jiaotong University as a PhD supervisor from 2008 to now. At present, professor Zhuang is the director of environmental monitoring and evaluating institute in Shanghai Jiaotong University, an evaluation expert of National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC), a referee of several publications in China and international. Research direction: environmental planning and evaluation, the research of immunoassay method for detecting environmental pollutions, environmental chemistry, etc.
Guangxin Yang A PhD candidate at Shanghai Jiaotong University from 2009 to now. Research direction: the research of immunoassay methods for detecting persistent organic pollutants.

Xianyin Ping An on-job PhD candidate at Shanghai Jiaotong University from 2010 to now; Work at East China Sea Fisheries Research Institute, Shanghai at present. Research direction: risk analysis of persistent organic pollutants in ecological environment.
